speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape four of the interview with Anna Reich. It's the 7th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was three story--

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, could you just tell me a little bit about the barracks and the living conditions that you were in at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could describe the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can just say the living condition was-- there was latrines where you did have to wash, whatever time of the year there was. And there was very primitive condition. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only the cold water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What I recall, there was only cold water. There was plenty of lice. And actually, because of spraying continuously the DDT, maybe that's what stopped the greatest epidemic of typhoid fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I remember seeing one man who was very happy to get a white shirt from the laundry, whatever laundry there was. How he got it? And then I come-- and the whole shirt was, like, moving. There was white lice on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the lice did always accommodate to the color of what the person did wear. They'd camouflage themself like that. And I was absolutely horrified. The whole shirt, virtually did have a movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said-- he was-- he was a primitive sort of fellow. Then I said, take off that shirt. Take off that shirt, you know? How did you deal with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the conditions were-- beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with the lice and the hygiene?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think they must-- they must have sprayed the DDT. And because it was not-- because it was the danger for the prisoners. It was also the dangerous for going further, you know? So a lot of DDT, they called, I think. Which today, it's even forbidden to use, because, I think they find it out since that it affects your bones. And it's very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in terms of washing, how often were you able to clean yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a matter of survival, really. Some insist-- and I was between them to wash yourself, under any circumstances, as often as possible, cold or not, because I understood-- and I couldn't stand any other ways, yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have an opportunity?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was these latrines together with the wash rooms. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We liked to get every opportunity to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you perhaps describe the layout of the camp in terms of the barracks that you've lived in, and whether you lived with your husband, and what the camp actually looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear. There was a barracks which [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one part was parallel-- vertical. And some of them was horizontal. What I recall, darling. Believe me, I worked on myself so hard not to remember. It was a terrible time. There was hanging-- there was hanging-- I would rather talk about things like which are vivid in my mind, you know, than about the things which are not so vivid in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was hanging young boy. And we already have to watch a parade while they was hanging him, you know? Do you know why?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a stupid reasons. He did have some part of chicken, or some chicken, or something like that. And I remember we would have to walk and look in the direction of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they was hanging. What do you call it in English? Gallows, yes? And I remember walking, and there was one German. And they used to have some German soldier at this time, as well. Or maybe-- but he said to me, when I was passing by he said to me, don't look. You're too young. That, I recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that, perhaps--

speaker: interviewee
text: One in a million.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only time you got anything human?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also I recall-- which I forgot to tell you before-- in a ghetto, there was a Wehrmacht man. I don't know. They was allowed to inspect the ghetto, or what? He came to our-- where I did live. And he said, you know, I could send you to my mother in Germany. He said-- of course, I didn't trust him. I was suspicious.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, I says, what about my husband? He said, no-- he said, only you. Of  course, I didn't take advantage of it. But maybe he-- I think it's worth my mentioning. Maybe he-- I don't know. It was a freak. Why do you think that he chose to be nice to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know by what he was moved. Maybe he liked my looks. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were the two times that you got a human response?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was it. Yes. Once when this say, don't look-- you're too young. But I did have to look. But I could look down, instead of up, because I did have to turn my head. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was an average day in the camp like? Did you have roll call in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was an appell in the morning, early in the morning. We did have to come. And we have been counted. And despite what weather, we did have to be there on the appell. And there was inspection. If somebody did not come, of course, and they did find him, they was shot or punished. Or what-- mostly shot. There was one way of punishment-- [LAUGHS] IT wasn't by degrees, it was death sentence for everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, then, after the appell, we did go to the place where we worked. All depends. There wasn't such a regularity. There was sometime some other things, like watching punishments, watching beatings, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was soup. Where we was working, there was the soup. And you know, sometime, there was some food smuggled in. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the food smuggled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people was still working outside the concentration camp. And they did risk-- oh, I don't know, maybe some of the-- I'm not so well-informed on that, but maybe even some of those Ukrainians, they got something from the prisoners, or what, you know-- some contacts like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you at all aware of the Jewish calendar passing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mm-mm. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all normal life was suspended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, completely. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now could we talk about Schindler, and when you first got in contact with him, and how you became involved with working for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, as I said, the camp was liquidated. And towards the end, we was put on a train, you know, those famous trains--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was locked-- locked. And it wasn't very long journey from Krakow to Auschwitz, to Birkenau. I don't think it's a great distance, but it was very slow in the line. And then I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a rumor that we're going to Auschwitz, right? And later, I remember, when the train did stop on the sideline, and we have been let out. And I remember there was Jewish prisoners, so maybe the people which did have to help with all this procedure. And they all greeted us with-- they say, from here, is only one way, through the chimney. They kept on repeating this a few time.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a warning?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a warning? As information? As-- as a fact? And we did-- we have been in a hurry here and there a little knock. We have been rushed, what they called an [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be de-liced-- de-liced. See, they was always panicky about this epidemic. They wasn't worried about the prisoners, but they was always panicky that-- and we have been-- and then, we did have showers. We have been shaven-- everybody was completely shaven. And a lot of DDT again. I remember, I was smothered with that powder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that make you choke and cough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. It-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suppose you did try to breathe in between, you know? You did have to. And then, I remember there was some heap of funny clothes, and everybody tried-- because we was naked. After the shower was no towels or anything. So everybody did try to grab whatever they can from the heap of clothes. And I remember, because it was late autumn, I think it must have been October--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44? And I remember grabbing-- you know the Dutch clogs? There was a lot of the Dutch clogs. And I remember grabbing whatever I could. And I remember one was too small and one was much too big. And that I did have some sort of a coat. But at that stage, I really did think we're going to be exterminated-- going to be gassed. This was is very, very low morale.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Himmler was there, as well. Himmler was there, as well, on that occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I did see him twice, once in Plaszow and once in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see of him in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember the very fine-rimmed glasses he did wear, and black SS uniform, and very shaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a relaxed sort of-- relaxed way watching all this atrocities and whatever. I think he enjoyed the naked parades. I would say he did. Because twice, when I met him, that's was on the occasion with was the selection, and we have been naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think he arranged it so that he could be there when they were going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so. It must have been. Well, it made me think, because that was the occasion when I did see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at this stage--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just, did you think that Schindler had failed you and that you weren't going to get to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have been from this-- after this washing and grabbing the clothes, we're in a dark environment. It was raining and dark. And I've been cold, and hungry, and miserable. We have been rushed through the bed mad to some sort of barrack-- because it wasn't any light. It was one so very, very dim, dim light which did not illuminate anything, virtually. At that stage, I did think they're putting us in the crematorium. Because I remember, in the middle of this barrack, was like a long-- what I did think it was a chimney, you know, like some structure. And we have been rushed to lie down, lie down, lie down, with all the Germans out there, women, men. And I remember just falling down. At that stage, I gave up. At that stage, it was my lowest point. And even then, suddenly, to my biggest surprise, I heard noises. Appell! Appell! Appell! And I was amazed that I am still alive. That, I remember. We have been called out for the appell. We stand in this rain and cold-- terrible day. It was very bad weather. And then, they put us into diffident barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it was three-story. And very close, we did sleep. There wasn't much of the blankets or anything like that. And there, it was terrible hunger. Terrible hunger. And I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how much food did you have in a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called a coffee, which was like brown water, in the morning. And then what they called a soup-- it was just like another water, you know-- in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And! That was all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- yes. I remember I didn't eat four days. I was what you not call eating, yes? And then we was called in the appell, or somewhere. I really think I must have been sort of semi-conscious. But I did see, next to the barrack, must have been some sort of kitchen or something. I did see a cart of cabbages. And I did risk my life to just run and grab a cabbage. And I remember this bit of cabbage, so nobody would see me. And to my amazement, I digested raw cabbage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, it was another very terrible time when I was terribly hungry. And it was in the nighttime. And my friend, who did sleep next to me, she did find out that our friend-- I knew her too, her name was Felka Pleshovska, that she is working somewhere as a nurse close to Mengele, must have been, right? And she did find out that we are there, or something. Anyway, it was possibility for this friend of mine to see her. And she got her about four or five such potato-- [GERMAN], whatever-- potato balls, yes? It must have been from mashed potatoes or something. Anyway, I never forget that she did wake me up, and she say, have that. And I did have two. And I'm sure that saved my life, those two. This lady was working-- she did come to Auschwitz, as well. And they was asking, who is a nurse? And I believe she volunteered that she's a nurse. She wasn't a nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- when they-- when it came to the-- she must have worked with Mengele, I think, in this experimenting thing. Because when it came to her, she apparently told him, you know, I am not a nurse, but I want to live. And she stayed there. And after the war, I don't know. She was a witness to a lot of things in Israel, I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I understand--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. If we can just get back to Auschwitz and how long you were there for, and then, where you went after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't know how long I was there. I only know it must have been quite something, because I didn't have any periods at that time. Over eight months, I didn't have any periods.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- and in Schindler's-- and in-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you find out--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't menstruate.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Auschwitz? And how did you find out that you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then there was another selection, what I recall. It was, in my memory, they took us-- at one stage, they took us in what's supposed to be a shower. I'm telling you what I remember. What I did-- And it was-- they put few drops of hot water. And that did look, to me, like a gas chamber. I'm quite sure, until today, that I was-- at that stage, we have been in a gas chamber. And I remember one window on top. There was tiny little windows. And it was broken. And they took us out from there again. And it was another selection. And they was not only people which was on the Schindler list, apparently, because-- I don't know-- because was another selection. I remember this lovely girl. And she was very petite. And she did not, as the selection goes, that these people which are on that right look more like healthy-- healthier one. And she was chosen to the-- she was suggest to be to the left. And she tried to get-- smuggle herself to the right. And that German woman did notice it. And she did hit her. She opened her aorta, her vein. And she bled to death. That also stands very vivid in my memory. And then, after this selection-- I was so hungry and so down, this is really very bad for my memory-- because then, somehow, we was taken somewhere and the names was called out. And I was between the names. And then they took us to wagons. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think was happening at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they was saying, oh, Schindler, maybe with Schindler. Maybe Schindler. I don't know, but I did think maybe Schindler, you know? And that was terrible. Four days, I think, or more, we have been in these wagons without the food and without sanitation. And it was in wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in each car?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot of people in each car. A lot. And no sanitation. There was no water. And in a very, very bad way, I arrived. What they called-- we suddenly stopped-- Zwittau. And that's the name for [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Brinnlitz. It was just the station was Zwittau, but Brinnlitz was actually the-- the-- the place. And I remember, in this terrible outfit with one clog too small, which couldn't quite fit in, and one too big, and cold, and shaven. That's how I arrived at Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see when the doors opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the door opened, it was a room where they put us in. It was like a house, really. And there was a man's-- some part was separate. The men were separate, and the women. They brought us to one part. And we have been terribly exhausted. And there was only straw on the floor. And all I did want is to just lie down, fall to sleep on that straw. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet up with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband was apparently-- he must have been on the list, too. But he went to Gross-Rosen, not to Auschwitz. And somebody told him that I came, but he couldn't recognize me. And he come close. I remember-- I remember I did recognize him. He came close to the wire which separated us. And I remember a German did hit him with a-- what do you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: Baton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baton. Yes. That's of the gun, yeah? The baton in a shoulder. And that is my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like seeing him for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like seeing him for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a great relief that, by some strange sort of way, we found each other, right? And look-- there was those people in Schindler that were-- there was then work. We have been-- it was 4 o'clock, I think, in the morning, we did have to get-- there was appell every morning. And we was segregated to different work. And I was put what I-- on a polisher machine, grenade-- polishing grenade. But I'm sure those grenade didn't ever explode, because they was-- we was far from skilled people. I also remember, once, I bent down while the machine was going, the polishing machine. And it took a bit of my hair. And I was almost scalped. And I still have here a passage what is missing from that time, because it took out the hair with the roots.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the conditions in the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: The condition. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they better?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was this SS woman who did enjoy eating a bread roll with ham, which was to us then smelling like something out of this world. And I had been very hungry. And I'll never forget this, how I hated for her to eat it so ostentatious, like to give us the dig. But there was soup, and the soup was much thicker, in the evening. And there was a portion of bread, which was very much guarded, because it wasn't so much of. And the work-- I remember one young lady who was a great pianist. And she was put to work on the metal cutting machine. And well, being not skilled and whatever, it amputated her four fingers. It did-- she-- and I remember the tragedy that was for her, being a pianist, because she also was hoping to survive, that she would never be able to play piano.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it sounds like there were a lot of accidents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a typhoid fever when we came, because I remember-- [LAUGHS] I must have been a very strong person and have--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: --wonderful resistance. I had been, I think, 23. 23? Yeah. Must have had wonderful resistance, because this girl sleeping next to me, she did have typhoid fever, I remember. She was one lovely girl. She was a daughter of a doctor. And she was a lovely looking, gentle girl. And she was very feverish. And we were sleeping like sardines, you know, on this straw. And in the night, she did have a terrible urge, because diarrhea always associated with this complaint. And she used, what I understand, any vessel what was available. But she was self-conscious of it. And being so delirious, she didn't want to take it out to the latrine. But being so feverish, I remember, she spilled some of it. And I remember, she spilled over my legs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what can be more contagious than the feces of typhoid fever, you know? And I did not get it. I remember, I washed myself. Well, there was latrines. They was much better. You could keep cleaner. There was latrines to wash yourself. What was the general feeling there? Did you feel that you were safe now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Generally feeling there-- and I would like to-- I did see Schindler only from the distance. He was a good-looking man. And he never did display any aggressiveness towards people. And he was going, even if the German was there, he wasn't smiling and waving. But he was just walking, friendly expression, you know? But I remember very vividly Mrs. Schindler. And somehow, I did see her several time. And this girl, who did have the typhoid fever-- who, by the way, did not survive-- but she said-- she express always, before she dies, she wants to have a glass lamp. What she said, a lamp of white wine. And I heard, through Mrs. Schindler, she got it. She die-- she die.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I remember Mrs. Schindler always with a little basket covered. And she was giving whatever she could. She contribute, I think, quite a bit to our diet. I remember, once we have been terribly happy because a horse was killed in the-- I think it must have been where the front was coming closer, or whatever. And we used the horse meat for the soup. And the soup were thickened. Absolutely delicious. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We might end it there. --of these videos that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is the end of tape four of the interview with Anna Reich. It's the 7th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
